Um, thanks again for the opportunity to present today, um, on the issues facing the disability community following the March 18th, um, announcement, as well as everything that's come in between. Um, as a parent of autistic children and a member of the disability community, myself, I'm not here just for my own family, but on behalf of countless others who've been deeply affected by these changes, um, the independent review of disability support services highlights major systemic issues within FA Kaha.

Um, but I must emphasize that the report does not fully address the real world concerns of disabled individuals and their caregivers. Um, so it's, it's impacted in several ways. Um, they've severely restricted how families can use respite funding under the new guidelines.

Families are now limited to purchasing portable sensory items or tablets only like iPads. Um, these items can be beneficial for some disabled people, um, but for others it can actually be detrimental. Um, and the limitation is extremely rigid and fails to address the full range of needs within the disability community.

My children, um, have become more dysregulated with tablets, but what choice do I have? My previous forms of respite that provided myself with respite as well as my partner, um, and well as well as my children with respite are not able to be funded. Now I cannot work full time due to my children's needs.

And the only able, I'm re the only reason I'm able to work at all is because my husband works from home. Um, because we both need to be available for our kids' high needs. My husband spent five months unemployed this year because he needed a job where he could work from home and still be available. And this is the situation we are in, and that's just one family.

Before these changes, we have flexibility to purchase essential tools, sensory items, trampolines, sensory swings, blackout tents. Um, these aren't items that we necessarily purchased, but we had the flexibility to if we needed to. Um, and that created calm environments for those with sensory needs.

These items are crucial for many families, um, particularly who require portable and non-mobile supports to manage their behaviors and reduce anxiety. I can name many purchases that have been invaluable for our autistic autistic children and our family's wellbeing, but I'll give you one example. One of my autistic children cannot feel temperature.

They would shower with the water as hot as it would go if they weren't supervised because they enjoyed the sensory experience of the steam. Often they would come out of the shower with scolded skin and we couldn't let our child safely shower independently. My child was in the stages of puberty and found it incredibly uncomfortable to have me as her carer in the room while she showered, checking everything and making sure the water temperature wasn't changed.

Her dignity was absolutely compromised. Shortly after the Ministry of Health allowed funding to be flexible, we were able to install a lockable water temperature controller for hot water. We used individualized funding to purchase and install this, and it made significant impacts in our household. We could set a safe temperature for her to shower and she could still turn the shower handle to be all the way warm, get steam without the water scolding her.

She's now able to retain her dignity while she's in the shower. And this meets the enabling Good Life principles. This is an example of a purchase that would be considered an extra or ordinary cost to some people, but was a life-changing disability support to us and not something that would've been funded elsewhere in any other departments.

Furthermore, families were previously able to cover costs for respite, like staying in a hotel or providing their cares to come along with them and stay in a hotel. And that's changed as well. So disabled individuals can no longer use their funding to pay for a care to travel with them when going out of region. For disabled people who need Constant Care, this change severely restricts their ability to travel for medical appointments, specialist services, or even family visits.

Um, the care is support rates. Um, I would consider exploitative, um, $80 for 24 hours of care is ridiculous. The rate's shockingly low and you're never gonna attract proper carers who can care for disabled people at that rate. Um, I know there's other ways to be funded for respite or for personal cares, et cetera, but I just wanted to add that in there because, um, it's disgustingly low.

Um, the report also, this is the independent review, acknowledges that fa kaha is under immense financial pressure, but it fails to recognize the real emotional and mental toll these changes have on caregivers. Rural families are also affected disproportionately where they can't get cares or they can't get access to specialist services and would have to travel at great lengths.

There's also negative impacts on specialist services, public services. They keep telling us that there's public services available and there realistically is not. I'd be really interested to see the amount of people who get referrals to specialist services, such as occupational therapy, speech language therapy, and how many actually get accepted and how many get declined.

Um, my children, they need occupational therapy, they need psychologists, but everything is a coaching model. And we used to previously be able to use our funding to have one-on-one support, which we cannot access publicly. I can't be a psychiatrist, psychologist, occupational therapist, speech language therapist, behavioral therapists, pediatrician, dietician, a teacher as well as a parent.

It's not possible. I can't be all those things to my children and I'm not the only one person saying this. Professionals as well as other carers say this according to the Carers report, it completely undermines the enabling Good Lives principle. And most alarmingly is the lack of consultation that was, had these changes happened overnight without any consultation, um, to the dis disability community.

And, um, that is probably the most, has caused the most mistrust for us as the disabled community, um, between us and any part of the government to be honest. Um, because we don't know what's going to change overnight anymore. Um, they should have, if they wanted to take away those changes or reverse them in a way, they should have consulted people and given them notice.

Um, they say it wasn't because of finances, but they can say that all they want. It clearly was. Um, so what I would like is for the previous guidelines to be reinstated while a proper process is followed where they engage in proper consultation, um, with agencies, care agencies, um, and the disabled people themselves and preserve the enabling good life approach.

Um, the changes to the funding have caused immeasurable harm to families like mine. The flexibility we had allowed us to provide the best care for our loved ones while maintaining our mental and physical wellbeing. Without it, many families are at the risk of burnout if they aren't already.

Um, ours is definitely on that side of being burnt out. Um, and this affects the disabled cares, the disabled individuals that we provide care for. Um, and in turn, all of society will suffer, um, if we can't work and we can't do the things we would normally do.
